text:
RADIOLOGY REPORT

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
MRN: [on file]
ORDERING PHYSICIAN: Medical Oncology
STUDY: (1) Technetium-99m methylene diphosphonate (Tc-99m MDP) whole-body bone scintigraphy with SPECT/CT of the pelvis and lumbar spine; (2) CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with intravenous contrast
CLINICAL INDICATION: 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, Grade Group 5), germline and somatic BRCA2 biallelic loss, currently on olaparib 200 mg orally twice daily (dose-reduced from 300 mg due to anemia). Treated with prior ADT/abiraterone and enzalutamide. On denosumab for bone health. Restaging at approximately 6 months of PARP inhibitor therapy. PSA at time of imaging: 3.9 ng/mL. Current hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL (recent dose reduction for Grade 2/3 anemia).

COMPARISON: Tc-99m bone scintigraphy and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed approximately 4 months prior (at time of enzalutamide progression/olaparib initiation), and PSMA PET/CT performed at time of enzalutamide progression demonstrating multifocal osseous metastases and bilateral pelvic lymph nodes.

---

PART 1: Tc-99m MDP WHOLE-BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY WITH SPECT/CT

TECHNIQUE: Approximately 25 mCi of Tc-99m MDP was administered intravenously. Whole-body planar images were acquired in the anterior and posterior projections at approximately 3 hours post-injection. Supplemental SPECT/CT images were obtained of the lumbar spine and pelvis for anatomic correlation.

FINDINGS:

SKULL AND CERVICAL SPINE: No abnormal areas of tracer uptake. Degenerative changes noted at the C5-C6 and C6-C7 levels with mild increased uptake in keeping with spondylotic disease.

THORACIC SPINE: No new abnormal foci. Physiologic uptake is symmetric throughout the thoracic spine. No convincing evidence of active osseous metastatic involvement in the thoracic spine at this time. Mild background osteopenic change.

LUMBAR SPINE:
- L3 vertebral body: Previously identified focus of increased tracer uptake is now notably diminished and demonstrates a more homogeneous, mildly increased pattern with corresponding SPECT/CT showing dense sclerotic change replacing the prior lytic/mixed component. This appearance is most consistent with healing/treatment response in the setting of ongoing systemic therapy. The lesion is appreciably smaller in metabolic extent compared to the confirmatory bone scan performed prior to olaparib initiation.
- L4 vertebral body: Similarly, the previously documented focus of tracer uptake at L4 has markedly decreased in intensity and extent. SPECT/CT correlation demonstrates predominantly sclerotic matrix, consistent with healing osseous metastasis. This represents a meaningful improvement from the prior scan.

PELVIS AND SACRUM:
- Right iliac crest: The previously noted area of tracer uptake has decreased in intensity and now displays a pattern consistent with sclerotic maturation, indicating favorable treatment response. No extension of disease to the adjacent ilium is identified.
- Right sacral ala: The focus at the right sacral ala appears markedly diminished relative to prior studies, now approaching background activity. Residual low-level tracer retention may represent healing bone remodeling.
- Left acetabulum: Previously documented on confirmatory bone scan at enzalutamide progression as a new lesion with elevated tracer uptake. On the current study, tracer uptake at the left acetabulum is substantially reduced, no longer appearing as a conspicuous hot focus. Sclerotic change is seen on the low-dose SPECT/CT component, indicative of treatment response.

FEMUR:
- Right femoral neck/intertrochanteric region: Previously identified as a new lesion at the time of enzalutamide progression. Current study demonstrates a meaningful reduction in tracer uptake at the right femoral neck and intertrochanteric region. The lesion is less intense and smaller in extent on planar imaging. SPECT/CT shows evolving sclerotic changes. This is consistent with partial response to olaparib.

RIBS: Scattered mild symmetric uptake in the posterior ribs consistent with degenerative and mechanical changes. No convincing new focal hot spots to suggest new osseous metastatic involvement. No rib lesion at the left posterior 6th rib at this time (note: this lesion will appear on subsequent imaging; the subtle focus seen on this scan is not clearly abnormal at this timepoint).

LONG BONES AND APPENDICULAR SKELETON: No new focal abnormalities identified in the humeri, forearms, hands, tibiae, fibulae, or feet. No superscan physiology.

OTHER INCIDENTAL FINDINGS ON BONE SCAN: Mildly increased uptake in the right knee, likely degenerative. Normal renal excretion bilaterally. Bladder activity present (post-void imaging performed).

SPECT/CT ADDENDUM (lumbar spine/pelvis): Dense sclerotic changes in the previously lytic metastatic foci at L3, L4, right iliac crest, left acetabulum, right iliac crest, and right femoral neck/intertrochanteric region. No cortical breakthrough or associated soft tissue mass. No pathologic fracture identified at any known metastatic site.

IMPRESSION (BONE SCAN):
1. Marked overall improvement in osseous metastatic burden compared with prior studies. Previously identified lesions at L3, L4, right iliac crest, right sacral ala, left acetabulum, and right femoral neck/intertrochanteric region all demonstrate substantially reduced tracer uptake and evolving sclerotic change, consistent with partial-to-near-complete treatment response to olaparib in BRCA2-deficient metastatic CRPC.
2. No new bone lesions identified on whole-body planar scintigraphy or SPECT/CT.
3. Background degenerative changes in the cervical and thoracic spine as described.

---

PART 2: CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following administration of 100 mL of iohexol (Omnipaque 350) intravenous contrast. Axial images acquired at 1.25 mm slice thickness with coronal and sagittal multiplanar reconstructions. Post-processing performed on dedicated workstation.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear. No pulmonary nodules, consolidation, ground-glass opacities, or pleural effusions. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No endobronchial lesions. No pneumothorax. The previously identified area of clinical concern for possible early pulmonary involvement (noted by the treating oncologist) is not corroborated by CT at this time — no discrete pulmonary nodule is identified.

Mediastinum and Hila: Mediastinal contour is normal. No pathologically enlarged mediastinal or hilar lymph nodes. Heart size is normal. No pericardial effusion. Aorta and great vessels are unremarkable. Mild coronary artery calcifications, consistent with patient age.

Chest Wall and Pleura: No pleural effusion or pleural thickening. Osseous structures of the chest wall show no new lesions. Mild kyphosis in the thoracic spine, consistent with age-related change and known sclerotic foci.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with smooth contour. The hepatic parenchyma enhances homogeneously in portal venous phase. No focal hepatic lesions are identified on this examination. No biliary ductal dilatation. The previously discussed PSMA-avid hepatic lesion is not visualized as a discrete CT-detectable abnormality at this time, consistent with subcentimeter or resolved disease.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: Gallbladder is present without stones or wall thickening. Common bile duct is not dilated.

Spleen: Normal in size and attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Pancreas is normal in morphology and enhancement, without ductal dilatation or focal masses. Given germline BRCA2 status, the pancreas has been carefully evaluated — no pancreatic abnormality is noted.

Kidneys and Adrenal Glands: Both kidneys are normal in size, position, and enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. Simple bilateral cortical cysts (largest right kidney, 1.2 cm). Adrenal glands are normal bilaterally without nodularity, thickening, or suspicious enhancement. This is notable given prior abiraterone use.

Retroperitoneum: No enlarged retroperitoneal lymph nodes by size criteria. No free fluid.

Gastrointestinal: Stomach and small bowel appear normal without obstruction. Colon is unremarkable without evidence of mucosal thickening, mass, or diverticulitis. No significant stool burden or obstruction.

PELVIS:

Prostate and Seminal Vesicles: The prostate gland is enlarged, measuring approximately 4.6 cm in greatest dimension, consistent with known history of treated prostate adenocarcinoma. No new dominant intraprostatic lesion is identified by CT criteria (CT is known to be limited for intraprostatic disease evaluation).

Lymph Nodes: Right external iliac lymph nodes — previously enlarged at 0.8 cm at the time of enzalutamide progression (on prior PSMA PET/CT). On the current CT study, the right external iliac chain nodes measure up to 0.4 cm in short axis, consistent with meaningful reduction in nodal burden. No pathologically enlarged pelvic lymph nodes by size criteria. The previously identified left pelvic lymph node is no longer clearly visible. Right and left obturator and internal iliac chains are normal.

Bladder: Normal wall thickness and contour. No intraluminal filling defects.

Musculoskeletal: Sclerotic changes in the known metastatic foci of the pelvis (right iliac crest, left acetabulum, right femoral neck) consistent with treated disease as described on bone scan component. No new lytic or mixed lesions identified. No pathologic fracture.

---

OVERALL IMPRESSION:

1. PARTIAL RADIOGRAPHIC TREATMENT RESPONSE: Compared with imaging at the time of olaparib initiation (approximately 6 months prior), there is a convincing and substantial reduction in osseous metastatic burden across all previously identified lesions. Lesions at L3, L4, right iliac crest, right sacral ala, left acetabulum, and right femoral neck/intertrochanteric region demonstrate markedly decreased tracer uptake on bone scintigraphy, with SPECT/CT confirming sclerotic maturation. This pattern is consistent with a favorable osseous response to olaparib in the setting of BRCA2 biallelic loss.

2. NO NEW OSSEOUS LESIONS: Whole-body bone scintigraphy with SPECT/CT reveals no new foci of suspicious tracer uptake to suggest additional bone metastases at this time.

3. NO VISCERAL METASTATIC DISEASE: CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrates no hepatic, pulmonary, adrenal, or other visceral metastases. The liver is homogeneous without focal lesions. No pulmonary nodules.

4. NODAL RESPONSE: Previously enlarged right pelvic lymph nodes have decreased appreciably, now at the lower limit of normal by size (0.4 cm), consistent with nodal response.

5. CORRELATE WITH PSA AND CLINICAL STATUS: PSA 3.9 ng/mL at time of scan represents a significant decline from the pre-olaparib level of 18.2 ng/mL, corroborating the imaging response.

Dictated and signed by: Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging and Nuclear Medicine
Reviewed by: Nuclear Medicine attending (bone scan component)
